concepts.title: Properties of alkynes
concepts.conceptBody:
Alkynes

Functional group suffix $=-$yne ( review )

Disubstituted alkynes, $\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{R}$ ', are described as "internal" alkynes

Monosubstituted alkynes, $\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{H}$, are described as "terminal" alkynes.

1. Stability

  • As with alkenes, the more highly substituted internal alkynes are more stable.
  • By comparing thermodynamic data of alkynes and alkenes, it can be seen that the "extra" $\pi $ bond in an alkyne is weaker than the alkene $\pi $ bond.
  • The alkyne functional group consists of two sp hybridised $\mathbf{C}$ atoms bonded to each other via one $\sigma $ and two $\pi $ bonds.
  • The $2\pi $ bonds are produced by the side-to-side overlap of the two pairs of p-orbital not utilised in the hybrids.
  • The substituents are attached to the $\text{C}\equiv \text{C}$ via sigma bonds.
  • The $2C$ of the $C\equiv C$ and the 2 atoms attached directly to the $C\equiv C$ are linear.
  • Since alkynes are linear, they cannot exist as cis-/ trans-isomers.

2. Physical Properties

  • As with hydrocarbons in general, alkynes are non-polar and are insoluble in water but soluble in non-polar organic solvents.
  • Liquified acetylene or when compared, in explosive, burns with luminous smoky flame due to high carbon content, hence used for lighting purpose.
  • In general bond length increases in the order

$\text{C}\equiv \text{C}<\text{C}=\text{C}<\text{C}-\text{C}$

3. Reactivity

  • The $\pi $ bonds are a region of high electron density so alkynes are typically nucleophiles.
  • Alkynes typically undergo addition reactions in which one or both of the $\pi $-bonds are converted to new $\sigma $ bonds.
  • Terminal alkynes, $\text{R}-\mathbf{C}\equiv \mathbf{C}-\text{H}$, are quite acidic for hydrocarbons, $\text{pKa}=26$
  • Deprotonation of a terminal acetylene gives an acetylide ion.
  • The acetylide ion is a good nucleophile and can be alkylated to give higher alkynes.

4. Acidity of Terminal Alkynes

\[R-C\equiv C-H\xrightarrow[{}]{B{{:}^{-}}}R-C\equiv C{{:}^{-}}+\,B-H\]

Terminal alkynes are unusual for simple hydrocarbons in that they can be deprotonated $(\text{pKa}=$ 26) using an appropriate base (typically $\text{NaN}{{\text{H}}_{2}},\text{pKa}=36$ ) to generate a carbanion which can function as a $\text{C}$ centered nucleophile and so allow for the formation of new $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bonds by reacting with $\text{C}$ centered electrophiles (such as alkyl halides).

In order to appreciate what makes the terminal alkyne more acidic than most other hydrocarbons, we should look at the stability of the conjugate base (i.e., the carboanion).

For each type of carbanion shown, the nature of the hybrid orbital containing the electron pair is important. Increased $\text{s}$ character $\left( \text{sp}=50\%,\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}=33\% \right.$ and $\left. \text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}=25\% \right)$ implies that the alkyne $\text{sp}$ orbital is closer to the nucleus and so there is greater electrostatic stabilisation of the electron pair. Therefore the conjugate base of the alkyne is the most stable and the most readily formed. However the terminal alkyne $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bond is not strongly acidic and a strong base, such as the amide ion, $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}^{-}$, is required to form the carbanion.

concepts.title: Alkylation of acetylene and terminal alkynes
concepts.conceptBody:
Alkylation of acetylene and Terminal Alkynes

  • This method is used to prepare higher alkynes
  • Terminal alkyne is one that has one$-\text{C}\equiv \text{CH}$ at the end of the chain.

$\text{HC}\equiv \text{CH}+\text{Na}\xrightarrow{Liquid\,\,N{{H}_{3}}}\text{HC}\equiv \text{CN}{{\text{a}}^{\oplus }}\xrightarrow{RX}\text{HC}\equiv \text{CR}$

$\text{RC}\equiv \text{CH}+\text{Na}\xrightarrow{Liquid\,\,N{{H}_{3}}}\text{RC}\equiv \text{CN}{{\text{a}}^{\oplus }}\xrightarrow{RX}\text{RC}\equiv \text{C}{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}$

terminal alkyne

$C{{H}_{3}}C\equiv CH\xrightarrow[(ii)\,\,C{{H}_{3}}C{{H}_{2}}I]{(i)\,\,Na\,\,Liquid\,\,N{{H}_{3}}}\underset{\text{2-pentyne}}{\mathop{C{{H}_{3}}C\equiv CC{{H}_{2}}C{{H}_{3}}}}\,$

  • Above reaction shows that acetylinic and terminal$H$are reactive hence salt formation is easier.
  • RX and RX should be 1 alkyl halides, since higher 2 and 3 halides give mainly alkenes when they react with sodium salt of alkynes.

Alkylation can also be done using Grignard reagent

$\text{HC}\equiv \text{CH}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{Mg}-\text{Br}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CH}\equiv \text{C}-\text{Mg}-\text{Br}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$

$\text{HC}\equiv \text{C}-\text{Mg}-\text{Br}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{Br}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{HC}\equiv \text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{MgB}{{\text{r}}_{2}}$ 

Example

Outline a synthesis of propyne from isopropyl or propyl bromide. The needed vic-dihalide is formed from propene, which is prepared from the alkyl halides.

Solution: 

Example

Synthesize the following compounds from $\text{HC}\equiv \text{CH}$ and any other organic and inorganic reagents (do not repeat steps):

(a) 1-pentyne, (b) 2-hexyne

Solution: 

$\text{(a) H}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{H}\xrightarrow{NaN{{H}_{2}}}\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv \overset{-}{\mathop{\text{C}}}\,:\overset{+}{\mathop{\text{N}a}}\,\xrightarrow{C{{H}_{3}}C{{H}_{2}}C{{H}_{2}}I}\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

$\text{(b) }\overset{+}{\mathop{\text{N}a}}\,:\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{H}\xrightarrow{C{{H}_{3}}I}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{H}\xrightarrow{NaN{{H}_{2}}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}\equiv \overset{-}{\mathop{\text{C}}}\,:\overset{+}{\mathop{\text{N}}}\,\text{a}$

$\xrightarrow{C{{H}_{3}}C{{H}_{2}}C{{H}_{2}}I}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

6. Alkylation of Alkynes

$\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{H}\xrightarrow[2.R'-X]{1.NaN{{H}_{2}}}\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv -{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}$

  • Terminal alkynes are usual for simply hydrocarbons in that they can be deprotonated ($\text{pKa}=26$) using an appropriate base (typically$\text{NaN}{{\text{H}}_{2}},\text{pKa}=36$) to generate a carbanion.
  • 3 alkyl halides are more likely to undergo elimination.
  • One or both of the terminal$\text{H}$atoms in ethylene (acetylene)$\text{H}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{H}$ can be substituted providing access to monosubstituted $(\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-\text{H})$ and symmetrical $\left( \text{R}={{\text{R}}^{\prime }} \right)$ or unsymmetrical $\text{R}\ne {{\text{R}}^{\prime }}$ ) disubstituted alkynes $\text{R}-\text{C}\equiv \text{C}-{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}$.

Mechanism
